one_line_takeaway: SELF-REDTEAM trains a single LLM to red-team itself via online zero-sum self-play, co-evolving attacker and defender to cut attack success rates (up to ~95%) and broaden attack diversity while mostly preserving capability, though its Nash-equilibrium safety guarantee is theoretical and gains hinge on the guard model.
key_finding: Co-evolving attacker and defender in one policy produces both more diverse attacks and more robust defenses than training either role in isolation. On four RLHF-aligned models SELF-REDTEAM improves nearly all harmful-refusal metrics (average relative safety improvement ~33-45% per model family) with minimal over-refusal and largely preserved general capability. Against a safety-removed (abliterated) Llama3.1-8B-IT-AB it restores safety dramatically (up to ~96.6% ASR reduction on WildJailBreak vanilla-harm), supporting standalone use. Attack diversity is higher and does not collapse (unlike Attacker-Only), and defenses generalize to unseen dynamic jailbreaks (avg ~31.3% ASR reduction, up to 50.7% on PAIR). The authors also give judge-independent evidence (cross-judge training, independent dynamic judges, label-based benchmarks) that gains are not reward-hacking artifacts.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SELF-REDTEAM, presented as the first fully online self-play multi-agent RL (MARL) algorithm for LLM safety alignment. A single shared LLM policy (πθ) plays BOTH the attacker (rewrites seed prompts into stealthy adversarial queries) and the defender (responds to those queries), co-evolving continuously rather than in the conventional reactive attack-then-patch loop. Two distinguishing design choices are (1) Hidden Chain-of-Thought, where each role's <think>...</think> reasoning is private and excluded from opponent view and from reward scoring, and (2) a game-theoretic framing as a two-player zero-sum min-max game yielding Theorem 1: at Nash equilibrium the defender produces a safe response (r_ϕ ≥ 0) to any attacker prompt. Training uses the critic-free Re++ (REINFORCE++) algorithm with role-specific advantage normalization and an optional auxiliary self-distilled SFT loss to preserve conversational quality. NOTE: the paper positions itself against prior self-play work (SPIN, SPAG, SPPO, DPO-based DuoGuard, RNN-based Perez et al. 2022) as Related Work; those are cited baselines/context, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Adversarial prompting / jailbreaks against safety-aligned instruction-tuned LLMs. The attacker seeks to (a) elicit harmful responses to harmful queries, OR (b) induce inappropriate refusals of benign queries (over-refusal is treated as an attack success too). Adversary capability is prompt-level rewriting of seed harmful/benign behaviors into stealthier adversarial variants; evaluation additionally stresses the trained defender against external dynamic jailbreak methods (X-Teaming multi-turn agentic, PAIR iterative refinement, AutoDAN optimization-based). Safety is adjudicated by a guard/reward model (WildGuard-7B or Qwen3Guard) over three labels: query harmfulness, response harmfulness, response refusal.
limitations: (1) The headline theoretical safety guarantee holds only at exact Nash equilibrium and rests on the assumption that a bare refusal scores zero reward; the authors concede exact NE and zero empirical harmfulness are not achieved in practice, so Theorem 1 is aspirational rather than an operational guarantee. (2) Safety depends entirely on the quality of the guard/reward model; a weak or gameable judge would bound the achievable safety. (3) Capability trade-offs are model-family dependent: Llama3.1-8B-IT loses instruction-following (AlpacaEval-2 LC winrate -13.5%, and -17.1% on the abliterated variant), and Hidden CoT is essential to avoid severe over-refusal on Llama (CoT-free variants collapse to 0.528 / 0.470 XSTest compliance). (4) Benign-compliance ASR (adv-benign) slightly drops for most models (e.g., -2.6% to -18.8% on the abliterated model), showing a residual safety-vs-helpfulness tension. (5) Self-play costs ~44-48% more wall-clock time than defender-only RL due to online attack generation. (6) Dynamic-attack ASR remains high in absolute terms (e.g., X-Teaming 62-80% after training), so robustness is improved but far from solved.

datasets_used: WildJailBreak (26,000 RL prompts, 50:50 vanilla_harmful/vanilla_benign; also eval partitions)
Self-distilled SFT set of 30,000 examples (15k WildJailBreak benign + 15k HelpSteer3)
General-capability: IFEval, ARC-C, GPQA, MMLU, TruthfulQA-MC1
quantitative_results: +17.8% more semantically diverse attacks (inverse SBERT similarity) vs Attacker-Only
Up to 95% attack-success-rate reduction on RLHF-aligned off-the-shelf models
Abliterated Llama3.1-8B-IT-AB: WildJailBreak vani-harm ASR 0.553 -> 0.019 (+96.6%); WG:Test adv-harm 0.478 -> 0.138 (+71.1%)
Average ASR reduction 36.43% across 11 safety benchmarks (ablation, Qwen2.5-14B)
+17.33% additional safety robustness over Defender-Only+SFT, with higher AlpacaEval-2 LC winrate (38.09% vs 35.50%)
Dynamic attacks (Qwen2.5-14B-IT): X-Teaming 76.0->66.0, PAIR 0.218->0.108 (+50.7%), AutoDAN 0.170->0.105; avg ~31.3% ASR reduction across settings
Attack novelty: base-model perplexity on generated attacks 8.62 (Attacker-Only) -> 24.74 (Self-Play), ~3x increase
Effective attack-strategy count 4.6 (Self-Play) vs 1.9 (Attacker-Only); Attacker-Only collapses to one tactic covering 86.3% of attacks
Qwen3Guard judge vs WildGuard: improves 8/11 safety metrics, +4.0% avg relative gain
Compute overhead: self-play ~44-48% more time than defender-only; with SFT, 3h32m vs 3h35m (Llama3.1-8B-IT)
Capability cost on Llama3.1-8B-IT: AlpacaEval-2 LC winrate -13.5% (24.74 -> 21.41)
baselines_compared: Attacker-Only (RL attacker vs fixed defender; a la Perez et al. 2022)
Defender-Only (RL defender vs static attack data; standard safety post-training)
Self-Play + SFT (= SELF-REDTEAM, the full method)
Base instruction-tuned models: Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B-IT, Llama3.1-8B-IT, Llama3.1-8B-IT-AB
